affected_groups: Pupils and families in poorer or less-organized villages
Rural teachers, especially the aging multigrade-teaching workforce
Tankerület and municipal administrators conducting the case-by-case negotiations
Independent policy researchers seeking comparative evidence
content.decision_question: Equity and transparency versus administrative flexibility and local autonomy in negotiation
content.evidence_boundary: Contested and largely unknown: no comparative study of settlement-level negotiation outcomes currently exists, so whether ad hoc negotiation actually produces worse results for poorer villages versus a national framework is asserted from historical pattern-matching (teacher aging trend, rising vacancy rates) rather than demonstrated with negotiation-outcome data; the scenario's own uncertainty section flags this gap
content.tension: Retaining village schools via ad hoc, case-by-case negotiation without a national framework leaves outcomes unmonitored and risks leaving poorer, less-organized villages and their pupils invisible unless enrollment, cost, staffing and equity data are transparently disclosed and audited.
content.title: Decision tension A1: S1
value_poles: administrative flexibility and local autonomy in negotiation
